content.text: Lineage metadata standards for agentic applications are agreed-upon schemas, naming conventions, and interchange formats that define how lineage information is recorded, stored, and shared across the multiple components of an AI agent's stack. Rather than each team defining its own ad-hoc lineage fields, a metadata standard specifies exactly which fields must be present, what their names and types are, how they relate to each other, and which external standards (W3C PROV-DM, OpenLineage) they align with.

Standardised lineage metadata makes lineage records interoperable: the observability platform, the data catalog, the audit system, and the compliance dashboard can all consume the same lineage records without custom adapters. It also enables cross-agent lineage stitching—tracing a data item from its original source through multiple agent handoffs to its final use—which is impossible without a shared identifier scheme and field naming convention.

Agentic AI systems are rarely deployed as single isolated agents. In production, agents hand off to other agents: a planning agent delegates subtasks to specialist agents, whose outputs feed into an aggregation agent. Without shared lineage metadata standards, each agent's lineage records are disconnected islands. An auditor asking 'where did the data in this final report come from' cannot traverse the full chain if the intermediate agents used different lineage schemas or identifier formats.

As organisations accumulate multiple agentic applications, the lack of metadata standards creates data governance debt. Compliance teams must understand five different lineage formats to produce a single audit response. Data catalog tools cannot ingest heterogeneous lineage records without custom integration work for each application. A standard developed once—and enforced as a platform requirement—eliminates this debt before it accumulates.
Shared identifier schemes (session_id, lineage_id chains) enable tracing data across multi-agent workflows without manual correlation.
Standard-compliant lineage records can be consumed by data catalog tools (Apache Atlas, DataHub), observability platforms (LangSmith, Grafana), and compliance dashboards without custom adapters.
A single schema that all agents write means auditors learn one format once and can query all agents' lineage records with the same tooling.
Automated governance checks (tier escalation detection, out-of-region inference alerts) can be implemented once against the standard schema and applied to all compliant agents.

As agentic AI capabilities evolve, new lineage fields become necessary (e.g., multimodal input types, tool call trees). The standard must have a versioning and extension mechanism that does not break existing consumers.
Making a metadata standard effective requires enforcing it at the framework level. Voluntary compliance in a multi-team organisation tends toward divergence.

Existing data lineage standards like OpenLineage were designed for batch ETL pipelines, not real-time agentic inference. Adaptation requires extending the standard with AI-specific facets that may not be stable.
Teams tend to add custom fields to lineage records for application-specific needs. Without governance over the standard itself, custom fields proliferate and undermine interoperability.
Two external standards provide the conceptual foundation for agentic lineage metadata.

The W3C Provenance Data Model (PROV-DM) provides a universal ontology for provenance records. It defines three core elements: Entity (a thing that can be described), Activity (something that occurs and can use or generate entities), and Agent (something that bears responsibility for an activity). In agentic AI, the inference call is the Activity, context components (retrieved documents, tool outputs) are the Entities it uses, and the AI agent and user are the Agents. PROV-DM relationships—wasGeneratedBy, wasAttributedTo, wasDerivedFrom, used—provide the semantic vocabulary for expressing lineage relationships in a standards-compliant way.

OpenLineage is a Linux Foundation project defining an open standard for data lineage events in data pipelines. It introduces the concepts of Run (a specific execution of a Job), Job (a transformation or computation), and Dataset (an input or output data entity). While OpenLineage was designed for Spark, dbt, and Airflow pipelines, its facet extension mechanism allows AI-specific metadata to be attached to Run and Dataset events. Agentic applications can publish OpenLineage events for inference calls, treating the context assembly and inference as a Job run and the context components as input datasets.

attrs.alt: Four-layer lineage metadata model for agentic applications

content.text: session_id, agent_id, agent_version, user_id, jurisdiction, start_time
lineage_id, session_id, model_id, endpoint, input_tokens, output_tokens, max_data_tier
retrieval_id, lineage_id, source_doc_id, source_doc_version, similarity_score, data_tier
A metadata standard is only as useful as its identifier scheme. The following conventions ensure identifiers are unique, traceable, and compatible across systems.
session_id, lineage_id, retrieval_id, output_id are UUID v4 values. Avoid sequential integers, which create ordering dependencies and enumeration risks.
Prefix identifiers with their type (ses-, lnx-, ret-, out-) to make the type immediately readable in logs and dashboards without schema lookup.
user_id should be a one-way hash of the actual user identifier (HMAC-SHA256 with a per-tenant secret), never the plaintext username or email.
Source document IDs should be deterministic from content (SHA-256 of canonical document content + metadata) so that the same document always produces the same ID, enabling deduplication.
agent_version should follow semver (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH) to enable filtering by version range in lineage queries.
Any deployment where more than one agent processes data and hands off results requires a shared lineage identifier scheme to stitch the provenance chain.
When a platform team supports multiple application teams building agents, a metadata standard is the mechanism that makes platform-level governance tooling work without per-application customisation.
Audit requirements that ask for lineage evidence are easiest to satisfy when all agents write to the same schema—a single query satisfies the requirement for all agents simultaneously.
When integrating agentic lineage records with a data catalog (DataHub, Apache Atlas), a standards-aligned schema enables automatic ingestion without custom extractors.

Implement the metadata standard as a shared library that all agent frameworks in the organisation are required to use. The library provides the lineage record classes, identifier generation functions, and validation utilities. Enforcing the library as a dependency at the platform level prevents teams from implementing their own lineage schemas.
Define the metadata standard as a formal schema (JSON Schema or Protobuf) versioned in a shared repository.
Publish a shared library (Python package, npm module, Go module) that provides lineage record types conforming to the schema.
Add schema validation to the lineage event stream consumer: reject records that do not conform to the current schema version.
Provide a lineage SDK that wraps the shared library with helper functions for the most common patterns: session start, inference call, retrieval, tool call, output creation.
Publish the standard to the internal developer portal alongside documentation and worked examples.
Version the schema using semver and maintain a changelog. Breaking changes require a major version bump and a migration period.

"""
Lineage metadata standard implementation.
Schema version: 1.2.0
"""
import uuid, hashlib, hmac
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from enum import IntEnum
from typing import Optional

SCHEMA_VERSION = "1.2.0"

class DataTier(IntEnum):
    PUBLIC = 1; INTERNAL = 2; CONFIDENTIAL = 3; RESTRICTED = 4

def make_session_id() -> str: return f"ses-{uuid.uuid4()}"
def make_lineage_id() -> str: return f"lnx-{uuid.uuid4()}"
def make_retrieval_id() -> str: return f"ret-{uuid.uuid4()}"
def make_output_id() -> str: return f"out-{uuid.uuid4()}"

def hash_user_id(raw_user_id: str, tenant_secret: str) -> str:
    """Produce an opaque, consistent user identifier."""
    return hmac.new(
        tenant_secret.encode(), raw_user_id.encode(), hashlib.sha256
    ).hexdigest()[:16]

def _now() -> str:
    return datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()

@dataclass
class SessionMetadata:
    """L1 — Session-level lineage metadata."""
    session_id: str = field(default_factory=make_session_id)
    agent_id: str = ""
    agent_version: str = ""  # semver
    user_id: str = ""        # opaque hash
    jurisdiction: str = ""   # e.g. "EU", "US"
    start_time: str = field(default_factory=_now)
    end_time: Optional[str] = None
    schema_version: str = SCHEMA_VERSION

@dataclass
class InferenceMetadata:
    """L2 — Per-inference lineage metadata."""
    lineage_id: str = field(default_factory=make_lineage_id)
    session_id: str = ""
    model_id: str = ""
    endpoint: str = ""
    input_tokens: int = 0
    output_tokens: int = 0
    max_data_tier: DataTier = DataTier.PUBLIC
    latency_ms: float = 0.0
    timestamp_utc: str = field(default_factory=_now)
    schema_version: str = SCHEMA_VERSION

@dataclass
class RetrievalMetadata:
    """L3 — Retrieval provenance metadata."""
    retrieval_id: str = field(default_factory=make_retrieval_id)
    lineage_id: str = ""
    source_doc_id: str = ""
    source_doc_version: str = ""
    similarity_score: float = 0.0
    data_tier: DataTier = DataTier.PUBLIC
    index_id: str = ""
    timestamp_utc: str = field(default_factory=_now)
    schema_version: str = SCHEMA_VERSION

@dataclass
class OutputMetadata:
    """L4 — Output lineage metadata."""
    output_id: str = field(default_factory=make_output_id)
    lineage_id: str = ""
    output_type: str = ""  # e.g. "text_response", "tool_action", "report"
    data_tier: DataTier = DataTier.PUBLIC
    destination: str = ""  # e.g. "user_chat", "crm_write", "email_send"
    human_review_required: bool = False
    timestamp_utc: str = field(default_factory=_now)
    schema_version: str = SCHEMA_VERSION

# Example: complete lineage for one inference call
session = SessionMetadata(
    agent_id="customer-support-agent",
    agent_version="2.1.3",
    user_id=hash_user_id("user@example.com", "tenant-secret-abc"),
    jurisdiction="EU",
)

inference = InferenceMetadata(
    session_id=session.session_id,
    model_id="claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022",
    endpoint="https://bedrock-runtime.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com",
    input_tokens=8420, output_tokens=312,
    max_data_tier=DataTier.CONFIDENTIAL,
    latency_ms=1240.0,
)

retrieval = RetrievalMetadata(
    lineage_id=inference.lineage_id,
    source_doc_id="sha256:abc123",
    source_doc_version="3.0",
    similarity_score=0.92,
    data_tier=DataTier.CONFIDENTIAL,
    index_id="kb-v4",
)

output = OutputMetadata(
    lineage_id=inference.lineage_id,
    output_type="text_response",
    data_tier=DataTier.CONFIDENTIAL,
    destination="user_chat",
    human_review_required=False,
)

print(f"Session: {session.session_id}")
print(f"Inference: {inference.lineage_id}")
print(f"Retrieval: {retrieval.retrieval_id}")
print(f"Output: {output.output_id}")

A global insurance company operates six agentic applications: claims processing, underwriting support, customer communications, regulatory filing, internal HR Q&A, and fraud detection. The platform team standardised on a lineage metadata schema at the start of the rollout, enforced through a shared Python library published to the internal PyPI server. Each application team is required to depend on the shared library and is blocked from modifying the core lineage classes. Two years into production, the compliance team can query a single Athena lineage table and produce cross-application audit evidence in hours rather than days. When a regulatory inquiry arrived about the use of a specific document in a claims decision, the compliance team queried by source_doc_id and retrieved the complete chain: which claims processing agent session retrieved it, which inference call used it, which output it contributed to, and which claim file that output was attached to—all from the standardised lineage records.
Without enforcement, teams diverge immediately. The standard must be implemented in a shared library and enforced as a platform dependency.
Using free-text strings for data_tier values (e.g., 'confidential', 'Confidential', 'CONFIDENTIAL') creates inconsistency. Use integer enums that map to canonical tier names.
Without a schema_version field in every record, it is impossible to handle records written under different schema versions when consuming historical data after a schema upgrade.
Teams that hardcode identifier formats (e.g., their own prefix conventions) create conflicts when the platform publishes a standard. Enforce identifier generation through the shared library.
The standard itself needs governance: a change process, a changelog, a version history, and a responsible team. Without this, the standard drifts or becomes stale.
JSON Schema enables automated validation of lineage records at the event stream consumer, catching non-conformance before records reach the lineage store.
Include a validate() function in the shared library that teams can call in tests to verify that their lineage records conform to the schema.
Minor version changes should only add optional fields; never remove or rename fields in a minor version. Breaking changes require a major version and migration path.
Maintain an alignment table for external standards:
Document explicitly how each field in the internal standard maps to W3C PROV-DM concepts and OpenLineage facets, enabling integration with external tooling without custom mapping logic.

Agentic AI capabilities evolve rapidly. Review the standard at least annually to add fields for new capabilities (multimodal inputs, agent-to-agent calls) before those capabilities reach production without lineage coverage.

The metadata standard should be designed for efficiency at scale. Smaller records write faster and cost less to store. Keep required fields minimal: every additional required field is a burden on all agent teams. Use optional fields for information that only some agents need. Use compact types: integer enums instead of strings, hashed identifiers instead of plaintext values, timestamps as ISO-8601 strings rather than objects.
For high-throughput event streams, Protobuf serialisation of lineage records is significantly more compact and faster to serialise/deserialise than JSON.
Design the lineage store schema with partitioning and pruning in mind: partition by date and session_id for efficient time-range and session-scoped queries.

Marquez (OpenLineage-native lineage backend), Apache Atlas, DataHub all support OpenLineage-compatible events; an agentic lineage standard that publishes OpenLineage events integrates with these tools without custom adapters.
JSON Schema for schema definition; Pydantic for Python type-safe lineage record classes that validate on construction.
Schema registry-backed serialisation format that enforces schema compatibility across versions, preventing incompatible schema changes from reaching the event stream.
Open-source data catalog with a lineage graph UI that can visualise cross-agent lineage if records are ingested in a compatible format.

The lineage metadata standard itself can be a security liability if it exposes sensitive field values. Field-level privacy must be built into the standard: user_id is always a hash, query_text is hashed for high-tier sessions, content fields are never included. The standard should document which fields may contain sensitive data and what treatment is required.

Schema evolution is a reliability concern: a consumer that reads lineage records from a previous schema version without handling schema differences will fail silently or produce incorrect results. Implement schema evolution using the additive-only principle and consumer-side schema version checks that log warnings for records from future schema versions rather than failing hard.
Lineage metadata standards define the schema, naming conventions, and identifier formats that make lineage records from multiple agents interoperable.
The four-layer model—Session, Inference, Retrieval/Tool, Output—covers the complete data path of an agentic inference call.
Enforcement through a shared library is the only reliable mechanism for achieving cross-team schema compliance in a multi-team organisation.
Alignment with W3C PROV-DM and OpenLineage enables integration with external data catalog and lineage visualisation tools without custom adapters.
Schema versioning with additive-only minor changes and a formal change process is essential for maintaining the standard as agentic capabilities evolve.
